title: Choosing a Chunking Strategy
concept: Matching chunking approach to document type: prose favors semantic, logs/code favor fixed or structural splitting.

exercises.prompt: How does the 'Recursive Chunking' method we learned in the last lesson decide where to split text?

options.text: It only splits text at random character counts without looking at punctuation.
It tries to split on paragraph breaks first, falling back to sentences and words if the chunk is still too large.
It uses an AI model to rewrite sentences so they fit exactly into 100 characters.

exercises.explain: Recursive chunking works by trying to split on large separators first (like paragraphs), and incrementally falling back to smaller ones (sentences, then words) only if the chunk size limit is exceeded.

cards.title: Right Tool, Right Job
cards.body: Not all data is created equal! If you chunk a textbook the same way you chunk server error logs, your AI assistant will get confused. Let's learn how to match the chunking tool to the job.

cards.title: Strategic Chunking
cards.body: Choosing a chunking strategy means selecting the split method that best preserves the meaning of your specific document type. Prose needs context-based splits, while structured files need rule-based or character-based boundaries.

cards.title: The Suitcase Rule
cards.body: Think of chunking like packing a suitcase. If you have soft, continuous clothes like sweaters (prose), you pack them by folding entire outfits together so they make sense. If you have rigid, identical blocks like Lego sets (logs or code), you pack them into rigid, equal-sized compartments to keep them structured.

cards.title: Code vs. Text
cards.body: If you chunk a Python script, splitting semantically on sentences will break the code logic. Instead, using a structural chunker that detects function definitions keeps the code runnable and understandable for the LLM.

cards.title: The 'One Size Fits All' Myth
cards.body: Many believe that semantic chunking is always superior because it is 'smarter'. In reality, semantic chunking is slow and often fails on logs, CSVs, or code, where fixed-size or structural splitters perform much better.

cards.title: The Chunking Matchup
cards.body: See how different document types align with different splitting paradigms to get the best results.

left.text: Semantic splitters keep the flow of arguments and stories intact.

right.text: Fixed-size or structural splitters preserve syntax and exact data lines.

exercises.prompt: Why should you avoid standard semantic sentence chunking for programming source code?

options.text: Source code does not contain any characters.
Code does not use human sentence punctuation, so semantic splitters will break functions apart.
Code is always too short to be chunked.

exercises.explain: Code has its own syntax and structure (like functions and classes). Structural chunking respects these boundaries, whereas semantic text chunking looks for human grammar rules that don't apply to code.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Fixed-size chunking is a poor choice for raw system logs.
exercises.explain: Fixed-size chunking is excellent for logs and raw data tables because it is fast, simple, and doesn't rely on human grammatical structures.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to select the best strategy for narrative text:
exercises.sentence: For a collection of personal essays and novels, you should use semantic chunking to keep complete thoughts together.
exercises.explain: Prose like essays, blogs, and fiction rely on narrative flow, making semantic chunking the best fit to avoid cutting off thoughts mid-sentence.

exercises.prompt: Which of these explanations about chunking a database log file sounds like it was written by an AI?

options.text: Since database logs are repetitive and structured by timestamps, a simple fixed-size character splitter is usually fast and effective.
Logs don't have paragraphs, so using a semantic splitter that looks for sentence boundaries is a waste of processing power.
Deploying a deep Neural Code Chunker optimizes the semantic tokenization paradigms of system outputs to achieve maximum vector alignment.

exercises.explain: The third option is generated by an AI that hallucinated a nonexistent 'Neural Code Chunker' tool and used overly complex, robotic phrasing to describe a simple programming concept.

exercises.prompt: You are building a dual-search engine: one part searches a library of fantasy novels, and the other searches computer system logs. How should you choose your chunking strategies?

options.text: Novels require fixed-size splitting while system logs require semantic splitting.
Novels favor semantic splitting to keep narrative ideas intact, while system logs favor fixed-size or structural splitting.
Both novels and system logs should always use identical semantic rules.
System logs cannot be chunked because they lack natural human grammar.

exercises.explain: Novels and essays rely on narrative flow, meaning sentences must stay with their paragraphs (favoring semantic or recursive splitting). System logs, on the other hand, are highly repetitive, structured, and don't have human-style narrative flow, making rigid fixed-size or structural splitting much more efficient and practical.
